lesson:
You wouldn't call your friend's new haircut 美しい. That's きれい territory. 美しい is reserved for things that deserve to be admired with distance and reverence — landscapes, music, prose. Five syllables (u-tsu-ku-shi-i) that roll out with a weight and deliberateness matching the word's register. It's formal, literary beauty.

美しい vs きれい: きれい is everyday beauty — pretty, clean, neat (yes, it also means "clean," which 美しい never does). 美しい is elevated — sublime, striking. 美しい景色 (beautiful scenery). 美しい音楽 (beautiful music).

The kanji 美: 羊 (sheep) over 大 (big). A big, fat sheep — the ancient Chinese ideal of beauty was abundance, vitality, something well-fed and thriving. That origin echoes in 美味しい (おいしい, delicious — literally "beautiful-tasting").

Dictionaryうつくしい
Negativeうつくしくない
Pastうつくしかった
Past negうつくしくなかった
Adverbうつくしく
て-formうつくしくて

Japanese has a clean spectrum of frequency adverbs, and いつも sits at the top: いつも (always, 100%) → たいてい (usually, ~80%) → よく (often, ~60%) → 時々ときどき (sometimes, ~40%) → あまり (not often, ~20%, + negative) → 全然ぜんぜん (not at all, 0%, + negative). Memorize this ladder and you can describe any frequency.

いつも is いつ (when) + も (also/every). When-also. Every time. いつも早はやく起おきる (I always wake up early). いつもありがとう (thanks, as always). 彼かれはいつも遅おそい (he's always late). It needs no particle — it works as a standalone adverb.

Where does the leg end and the foot begin? English draws a line at the ankle. Japanese doesn't bother. あし means both — the whole lower limb, one continuous structure. 足あしが長ながい can be a compliment about someone's long legs or a statement about their feet, depending entirely on context.

The kanji 足 was originally a picture of a knee joint with a foot below it — exactly the undivided view the word takes. As そく in compounds, it picks up an additional meaning: "sufficient." 満足まんぞく (satisfaction — being full-footed, having enough), 不足ふそく (shortage). A foot stepping toward a goal connects to reaching sufficiency.

When you need to be specific about just the leg: 脚あし (same reading) focuses on that portion — especially for furniture legs (テーブルの脚あし). But in everyday conversation, 足 handles everything.

子供を起こす (wake the child up). 事故を起こす (cause an accident). 革命を起こす (start a revolution). Three very different actions, one verb. The thread connecting them: you raise something from dormancy into existence. Waking someone up is just the most literal version.

起こす is the transitive half of a pair. 起きる (おきる) is intransitive — you wake up, things happen on their own. 起こす means you make it happen. You cause the rising. The kanji 起 — 走 (run) and 己 (self) — captures the energy of getting up and going.

Particles: 起こす takes を — the person or thing you wake/cause is your direct object. 子供を起こす. 問題を起こす.

Potential が起こせる: が marks what can be caused/woken. この音で子供が起こせる (this sound can wake the child).

Passive に起こされる: に marks who woke you. 朝6時に母に起こされた (I was woken at 6 AM by my mother).

Causative に起こさせる: に marks who you make do the waking. 姉に弟を起こさせる (have the older sister wake the younger brother).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 起きる (おきる)
Dictionaryおこすを
Negativeおこさないを
Politeおこしますを
Polite negおこしませんを
Pastおこしたを
Polite pastおこしましたを
て-formおこしてを
Volitionalおこそうを
Polite volおこしましょうを
Passiveおこされるに
Causativeおこさせるに／を
Caus-passおこさせられるに
Potentialおこせるが
Imperativeおこせを
Imp なさいおこしなさいを
Cond ～ばおこせばを
Cond ～たらおこしたらを

必ず合格してみせる — "I'll pass, just you watch." The pattern 〜てみせる, attached to another verb's て-form, is a declaration of intent with a hint of defiance. You're not just doing the thing — you're proving you can. Compare with 〜てみる (try doing to see what happens) — very different nuance despite the similar sound.

見せる on its own means "to show." Historically it was the causative of 見る (to see) — literally "make someone see." But it's been independent so long that it functions as its own verb now. 写真を見せる (show a photo). パスポートを見せてください (please show your passport).

Particles: 見せる takes を for the thing shown and に for the person you show it to. 友達に写真を見せる.

Potential が見せられる: が marks what can be shown. この書類が見せられない (I can't show this document).

Passive に見せられる: に marks who showed it. 怖い映画を見せられた (I was made to watch a scary movie).

Causative に見せさせる: making someone show something. Rare — 見せる already contains causative meaning.

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryみせるを
Negativeみせないを
Politeみせますを
Polite negみせませんを
Pastみせたを
Polite pastみせましたを
て-formみせてを
Volitionalみせようを
Polite volみせましょうを
Passiveみせられるに
Causativeみせさせるに／を
Caus-passみせさせられるに
Potentialみせられるが
Imperativeみせろを
Imp なさいみせなさいを
Cond ～ばみせればを
Cond ～たらみせたらを

うちの娘 — "my daughter." お嬢さん — "your daughter." Two completely different words for the same family member, depending on whose daughter you're talking about. 娘 is humble — you use it for your own children. For someone else's daughter, the respectful term is お嬢さん (おじょうさん). This humble/respectful split runs through all Japanese family vocabulary.

むすめ also means "young woman" in older or literary writing — あの娘 (that girl). The kanji 娘 is 女 (woman) plus 良 (good). A "good woman" meant a young, unmarried one in the society that created this character.

Its pair: 息子 (むすこ, son). Together they cover your children in humble speech. But there's an asymmetry — 娘 can mean any young woman, while 息子 only ever means "son."

The kanji 楽 started as a picture of a drum on a wooden stand — a musical instrument. From music came pleasure. From pleasure came ease. That's why 楽 has such range: 楽しい (fun), 楽 (easy/comfortable), 音楽 (music). They're all branches growing from a drumbeat.

楽しむ is the verb — to actively enjoy something, to choose to find joy. 旅行を楽しむ. 人生を楽しむ. Unlike the adjective 楽しい (which describes a state), the verb implies deliberate engagement.

楽しみにしている (I'm looking forward to it) — memorize this as a unit. It uses the noun form 楽しみ and is one of the most useful phrases in Japanese.

Particles: 楽しむ takes を — the experience is your direct object. 休みを楽しむ (enjoy the holiday).

Potential が楽しめる: が marks what can be enjoyed. ここで景色が楽しめる (the scenery here can be enjoyed).

Passive に楽しまれる: に marks who enjoys it. 世界中の人に楽しまれている (enjoyed by people worldwide).

Causative を楽しませる: commonly used as "to entertain" — お客さんを楽しませる (entertain the guests). Making someone experience enjoyment.

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryたのしむを
Negativeたのしまないを
Politeたのしみますを
Polite negたのしみませんを
Pastたのしんだを
Polite pastたのしみましたを
て-formたのしんでを
Volitionalたのしもうを
Polite volたのしみましょうを
Passiveたのしまれるに
Causativeたのしませるに／を
Caus-passたのしませられるに
Potentialたのしめるが
Imperativeたのしめを
Imp なさいたのしみなさいを
Cond ～ばたのしめばを
Cond ～たらたのしんだらを

The kanji 色 originally had nothing to do with color. It depicted a person kneeling over another — the posture of intimacy. Its oldest meaning was sexual desire, passion, beauty. The shift to "color" came through complexion: the flush of emotion on skin, the visual surface of feeling. That earlier meaning never fully left: 色っぽい (いろっぽい, sexy) and 色気 (いろけ, sex appeal) use 色 in its original sense.

As "color," いろ is the basic word. 何色？ (なにいろ, what color?). Colors attach directly: 赤色, 青色, 黄色 (きいろ — notably the only basic color requiring 色 even in its short form).

色々 (いろいろ, various) — literally "colors and colors," meaning many different kinds. One of the most common words in Japanese. Different colors, different types, all mixed together.

お菓子をみんな食べた — "ate all the snacks." Not "everyone ate the snacks" — all the snacks. みんな can mean "all of something," not just "all people." This trips learners up. It refers to the entirety of whatever it modifies, whether that's a group of people or a plate of cookies.

As "everyone": みんな来た (everyone came). みんなの意見 (everyone's opinion). The word comes from 皆 (みな) with an inserted ん for easier pronunciation. みな is slightly more formal; みんな is casual and warm.

The polite form 皆さん (みなさん) adds respectful さん. Teachers to classes, speakers to audiences, announcements to the public — all 皆さん. Friends to friends — みんな.

The kanji 取: 耳 (ear) plus 又 (hand). A hand grabbing an ear. This isn't metaphor — in ancient warfare, soldiers took enemies' ears as proof of kills. The kanji preserves that origin: to seize, to claim.

Modern 取る shed the violence but kept the breadth. It's one of the most overloaded verbs in Japanese: 写真を撮る (take a photo — 撮る is more specific). 休みを取る (take time off). メモを取る (take notes). 年を取る (age — "take years"). Physical objects, abstract concepts, spans of time — it doesn't care.

取る vs 撮る vs 採る: All read とる. 取る is the default. 撮る is for photos/video. 採る is harvesting or adopting measures. In casual writing, 取る or hiragana とる covers everything.

Particles: 取る takes を — the thing taken is your direct object. 塩を取って (pass the salt).

Potential が取れる: が marks what can be taken. 休みが取れる (I can take time off). Note: 取れる also means "to come off" — ボタンが取れた (the button came off).

Passive に取られる: に marks who took it. 猫に魚を取られた (the cat took my fish).

Causative に…を取らせる: に marks who you make take. 生徒にメモを取らせる (have students take notes).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryとるを
Negativeとらないを
Politeとりますを
Polite negとりませんを
Pastとったを
Polite pastとりましたを
て-formとってを
Volitionalとろうを
Polite volとりましょうを
Passiveとられるに
Causativeとらせるに／を
Caus-passとらせられるに
Potentialとれるが
Imperativeとれを
Imp なさいとりなさいを
Cond ～ばとればを
Cond ～たらとったらを

In Chinese, 勉強 (miǎnqiǎng) means "reluctant" — forcing yourself to do something you'd rather not. Japanese took that core sense and narrowed it to the most common thing people force themselves to do: study. The honesty is baked into the kanji: 勉 (exert oneself) + 強 (strong, to force). Studying as an act of willpower. The word doesn't pretend it's fun.

勉強する is a する-noun: 日本語を勉強する (study Japanese). You'll also hear 勉強になる ("it becomes studying" → I learned something, that was informative) — a polite way to acknowledge someone taught you something. Use it after receiving useful information and you'll sound appreciative.

meaning: to be able to, be completed
lesson:
できる is two verbs wearing one mask. 日本語ができる (I can speak Japanese) — ability. 準備ができた (preparations are done) — completion. 友達ができた (I made a friend — a friend came into being) — something materializing. The connection: if something can be done, it gets done. Ability and completion are two sides of one coin.

Technically できる is the potential form of する, but it's so independent that nobody thinks of it that way. It takes が, not を — the thing that's possible is the subject: 英語ができる. Same pattern as 分かる and 好き.

できるだけ早く (as fast as possible) — memorize this phrase as a block. "To the extent that can be done." Extremely common.

一段 Ichidan自動詞
Dictionaryできるが
Negativeできないが
Politeできますが
Polite negできませんが
Pastできたが
Polite pastできましたが
て-formできてが
Volitional
Polite vol
Passive
Causative
Caus-pass
Potential
Imperative
Imp なさい
Cond ～ばできればが
Cond ～たらできたらが

Mi-ji-ka-i — four syllables for a word meaning "short." Its opposite, 長い (ながい, long), has fewer syllables. Don't look for logic there, but it makes a decent mnemonic: みじかい is so long it overflows its own meaning.

短い covers length, duration, and distance. 短いスカート. 短い時間. 短い距離. But not height — a short person isn't 短い, they're 背が低い (せがひくい, low in stature). English speakers mix this up constantly.

The kanji 短: 矢 (arrow) plus 豆 (bean). An arrow as small as a bean — short.

Dictionaryみじかい
Negativeみじかくない
Pastみじかかった
Past negみじかくなかった
Adverbみじかく
て-formみじかくて

試験に落ちた — "I failed the exam." Not 失敗した (general failure). Exam failure is falling. And the opposite? 受かる (うかる, to pass) — to float up, to be accepted. You either fall or rise. Japanese frames academic fate as vertical motion.

落ちる is intransitive — things fall by themselves. Its transitive partner is 落とす (おとす, to drop). りんごが落ちる (an apple falls) vs りんごを落とす (drop an apple). 成績が落ちる (grades decline) vs 成績を落とす (let grades drop — you caused it).

The kanji 落: grass radical (艹) on top, water (氵) on the left. Rain dripping off leaves — water drops falling from foliage.

Particles: 落ちる takes が for the thing that falls. 葉が落ちる (leaves fall). For exams: 試験に落ちる — に marks what you fell at.

Passive: Rare in literal sense. For "making fall," use the transitive 落とす.

Causative: 落とす is used instead of the causative form in practice.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 落とす (おとす)

息 (breath) + 子 (child) = your breath-child. The child who carries your life force forward. 息 itself is 自 (nose/self) over 心 (heart) — breath flowing from nose to heart. Calling your son 息子 frames him as the continuation of your very breath.

Like 娘 (daughter), 息子 is humble speech — for your own son. For someone else's: 息子さん with the polite suffix, or more formally ご子息 (ごしそく).

An asymmetry worth noting: 娘 can refer to any young woman, but 息子 only means "son." It's locked to the parent-child relationship.

White is the color of death in Japan. Not black — white. Funeral garments, mourning fabrics, the color of the beyond. But white is also purity, honesty, confession: 白状する (はくじょうする, to confess — to make things white/clear). The color holds contradictions. It's the blank slate and the final page.

The kanji 白 is believed to show the sun just above the horizon — the first pale light of dawn, before color fills the sky. White as absence, as the moment before the world paints itself.

白い covers both the color and the concept of blankness: 白い紙 means both "white paper" and "blank paper." As a noun, drop the い: 白 (しろ). This noun/adjective pair exists for the four oldest colors in Japanese — 白/白い, 黒/黒い, 赤/赤い, 青/青い — the only colors with native い-adjective forms.

Dictionaryしろい
Negativeしろくない
Pastしろかった
Past negしろくなかった
Adverbしろく
て-formしろくて

飛 (fly) + 行 (go) + 機 (machine). A flying-going-machine. Japanese compound nouns can be remarkably literal, and this is a textbook case — no metaphor, no ambiguity.

You know the first two kanji already. The third, 機, means "machine" and shows up everywhere: 洗濯機 (せんたくき, washing machine), 機会 (きかい, opportunity — a mechanism of timing). When 機 sits at the end of a word, expect a device.

飛行機に乗る (board a plane — literally "ride") is the standard expression. Japan is an island nation; for international travel, there's no alternative to the 飛行機.

In traditional East Asian medicine, illness is a disorder of 気 — your vital energy. When 気 is blocked or depleted, you're sick. 病気 isn't just "disease" — it's sick energy, your life force gone wrong. 病 (sick) + 気 (energy) tells a story that goes deeper than "illness."

The kanji 病 has the sickness radical 疒 — a person lying on a bed. Every illness-related kanji uses it: 痛 (pain), 症 (symptom), 療 (treatment). Spot 疒 and you've found medical vocabulary.

病気になる (get sick — "become illness"). 病気が治る (recover). 病気で休む (take time off due to illness) — で marks the reason.

冬 is the season of 鍋 (なべ, hot pot), 温泉 (おんせん, hot springs), こたつ (heated table you put your legs under), and New Year. Japan's four seasons structure the culture in ways that go far beyond weather. Seasonal vocabulary matters in conversation — people expect you to talk about the seasons and how they shape daily life.

The kanji 冬 in its oldest form showed two knotted threads — endings, the tying-off of the year's cycle. The two dots at the bottom (冫) are the ice radical, added later. Ice under a symbol of closure — winter.

The four seasons as a set: 春 (はる), 夏 (なつ), 秋 (あき), 冬 (ふゆ). All single-kanji, native readings, ancient vocabulary. 冬休み (ふゆやすみ, winter vacation) — the break around New Year.

The kanji 年 was a person carrying a bundle of grain. One harvest cycle = one year. Time measured not by calendars but by crops.

とし means both "year" and "age," and the two blend seamlessly. 年を取る (to age — "take years"). 年が明ける (the new year arrives — "the year opens"). いい年して (at your age! — mild scolding). Time passing and growing older are the same word because they're the same thing.

Readings: とし for standalone use. ねん in compounds and as a counter: 三年 (さんねん), 去年 (きょねん), 来年 (らいねん). And the irregular one: 今年 (ことし — not こんねん). An old native reading that refused to be replaced.

tags: 重い vs 軽い pair
lesson:
Say おもい out loud. Feel how the word sits in your mouth — o-mo-i, three syllables that sink. Now say かるい (light). It bounces. Japanese speakers hear this too. The sound-feel isn't a coincidence — it's one of those places where the language just fits.

重い goes far beyond kilograms. 空気が重い — the atmosphere is heavy (tense). 気分が重い — your mood is heavy (depressed). 責任が重い — the responsibility is heavy (serious). 病気が重い — the illness is heavy (severe). And in dating: あの人は重い means someone is emotionally clingy — too much weight on the relationship. Every usage shares the same physical intuition: something pressing down on you.

The kanji 重 started as a picture of a person with a heavy bundle on their back, the strokes layering down like stacked weight. That layering sense survives in compounds: 重ねる (かさねる, to stack/layer), 二重 (にじゅう, double/twofold). Things that pile up get heavy.

おもい sounds identical to 思い (feelings, thoughts). Different kanji, same sound — and in classical Japanese poetry, heavy things and deep feelings were deliberately punned. A weight on the body and a weight on the heart were the same word on purpose.

Dictionaryおもい
Negativeおもくない
Pastおもかった
Past negおもくなかった
Adverbおもく
て-formおもくて

胸が痛い is not a medical complaint. It means your heart aches — you're emotionally hurt or moved. 胸がいっぱい (chest is full) means you're so overwhelmed with feeling you can't speak. 胸がドキドキする is your chest going thump-thump from nerves or excitement. Where English puts emotions in the "heart," Japanese puts them in the 胸 — the whole chest cavity, the space where you feel your pulse and your breath catch.

This matters because むね shows up in emotional vocabulary constantly. 胸を張る (puff out your chest) means to stand tall with pride. 胸に手を当てて考える (put your hand on your chest and think) means to search your conscience — the gesture and the idiom are one and the same. Physical and emotional aren't separate categories here.

The kanji has the meat/flesh radical 月 on the left — the same one that marks 腕 (arm), 肩 (shoulder), 腰 (waist). Spot that radical and you know you're looking at a body part.

払う originally had nothing to do with money. The core meaning is "to brush away, to sweep off" — clearing something unwanted. ほこりを払う (brush off dust). 手で虫を払う (swat away a bug). The jump to "pay" makes sense when you think about it: paying is clearing a debt, sweeping away what you owe. You're not giving money — you're removing an obligation.

That sweep-away sense stays alive alongside the money meaning. 注意を払う (pay attention — literally "sweep out your awareness"). 犠牲を払う (pay a sacrifice). The connecting thread: you're giving something up, sending it away from yourself. At a restaurant, ここは私が払う means "I'll cover this" — I'll be the one who clears the bill.

Particles: 払う takes を — the money or effort being cleared is the direct object. お金を払う. Add に for the recipient: 店に払う (pay to the shop).

Potential が払える: が — what you can pay becomes the subject. この金額が払える？ (can you pay this amount?).

Passive に払われる: に marks the payer. 給料が会社に払われる (salary is paid by the company).

Causative に…を払わせる: に marks who you make pay, を marks what. 後輩に払わせる (make the junior pay).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryはらうを
Negativeはらわないを
Politeはらいますを
Polite negはらいませんを
Pastはらったを
Polite pastはらいましたを
て-formはらってを
Volitionalはらおうを
Polite volはらいましょうを
Passiveはらわれるに
Causativeはらわせるに／を
Caus-passはらわせられるに
Potentialはらえるが
Imperativeはらえを
Imp なさいはらいなさいを
Cond ～ばはらえばを

tags: 重い vs 軽い pair
lesson:
If you've driven in Japan, you've seen 軽自動車 (けいじどうしゃ) — "kei cars," those tiny boxy vehicles everywhere, taxed at a lower rate because they're small and light. The 軽 in the name is this word. The kanji has the vehicle radical 車 on the left: a vehicle that's easy to pull, nimble. That's the physical anchor for the word — lightness as ease of movement.

Like 重い, 軽い stretches well beyond weight. 軽い食事 (a light meal). 軽い気持ち (a casual attitude — not taking things seriously). 軽い冗談 (a light joke). And the idiom that sticks: 口が軽い — literally "light mouth," meaning someone who can't keep secrets, who lets things slip. Lightness means ease but also carelessness.

Sound anchor: ka-ru-i bounces off the tongue — three quick syllables that feel effortless. Set it next to o-mo-i and you can hear the difference: one floats, the other sinks.

Dictionaryかるい
Negativeかるくない
Pastかるかった
Past negかるくなかった
Adverbかるく
て-formかるくて

You already know 見る (to see). 見つける adds つける (to attach, to fix onto) — seeing that sticks. Your eyes land on what you were looking for and lock there. Finding is seeing with grip.

The real lesson here is the pair. 見つける: you find something — you're the active searcher. 見つかる (みつかる): something gets found — it turns up. 鍵を見つけた (I found the key — I did it). 鍵が見つかった (the key turned up — it just appeared). Japanese strongly prefers 見つかる in daily speech because it avoids claiming credit. The thing showed itself; you didn't hunt it down. This preference for intransitive framing runs deep in the language.

You can also discover things you weren't looking for: 偶然見つけた (found it by accident). That's when 見つける gets closest to English "discover." And the pair with 探す (to search for): searching is the process, finding is the result. 探して見つけた (searched and found) — a natural sequence.

Particles: 見つける takes を — the thing you find is the direct object. 仕事を見つける (find a job).

Potential が見つけられる: が — what can be found becomes the subject. In practice, people just use 見つかる instead.

Passive に見つけられる: に marks the finder. 先生に見つけられた (I was found by the teacher — caught).

Causative に…を見つけさせる: に marks who you make find, を marks the thing. 子供に自分で答えを見つけさせる (make the kid find the answer themselves).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 見つかる (みつかる)
Dictionaryみつけるを
Negativeみつけないを

Polite negみつけませんを

て-formみつけてを
Volitionalみつけようを
Polite volみつけましょうを
Passiveみつけられるに

Caus-passみつけさせられるに

Imperativeみつけろを

Cond ～ばみつければを
Cond ～たらみつけたらを

The kanji tells the whole story: 亡 (to perish) over 心 (heart/mind). Something dies in your mind. That's forgetting. The same 亡 appears in 死亡 (death) and 亡くなる (to pass away). When you forget, the memory doesn't wander off — it perishes.

忘れる covers two things English keeps separate: forgetting information and physically leaving something behind. 名前を忘れた (forgot the name). 傘を忘れた (left my umbrella behind). In both cases, something that should be with you isn't. Japanese sees no need to distinguish mental forgetting from physical forgetting — the absence is the same.

The form that matters most: 忘れられない — "I can't forget." You'll hear this far more than the plain form. あの日のことが忘れられない (I can't forget that day). It's the potential negative, and it carries emotional weight — the memory won't die no matter what you do.

Opposite: 覚える (to memorize/remember) — word #130. 覚えたのに忘れた (I memorized it but forgot) is the eternal lament of language learners.

Particles: 忘れる takes を — the thing forgotten is the direct object. 約束を忘れる (forget a promise). For physical items left behind, add に for location: 電車に傘を忘れる (leave an umbrella on the train).

Potential が忘れられる: が — what can be forgotten becomes the subject. The negative 忘れられない is far more common.

Passive に忘れられる: に marks who forgets you. みんなに忘れられた (I was forgotten by everyone).

Causative に…を忘れさせる: に marks who you make forget, を marks what. 嫌なことを忘れさせてくれる (it helps me forget unpleasant things).

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryわすれるを
Negativeわすれないを
Politeわすれますを
Polite negわすれませんを
Pastわすれたを
Polite pastわすれましたを
て-formわすれてを
Volitionalわすれようを
Polite volわすれましょうを
Passiveわすれられるに
Causativeわすれさせるに／を
Caus-passわすれさせられるに
Potentialわすれられるが
Imperativeわすれろを
Imp なさいわすれなさいを
Cond ～ばわすれればを
Cond ～たらわすれたらを

In everyday Japanese, お酒 (with the honorific お) means any alcoholic drink — beer, wine, whiskey, everything. If someone asks お酒を飲みますか, they're not offering you a cup of nihonshu. They're asking if you drink at all. When you specifically want Japanese rice wine, say 日本酒 (にほんしゅ).

The kanji gives away its own history: 氵 (water) plus 酉 — a picture of a wine jar. Liquid from a jar. That 酉 radical marks almost every alcohol-related kanji in existence: 醸 (to brew), 酔 (drunk), 酢 (vinegar — fermentation that went too far). Once you recognize the jar, a whole family of characters opens up.

酒 carries real social weight. お酒の席 (a drinking occasion) is a recognized institution — after-work drinking, 飲み会 (のみかい), is where the real opinions come out and the real bonds form. Two of the first things Japanese people learn about each other socially: お酒に強い (can hold your liquor) or お酒に弱い (you're a lightweight). The に marks what you're strong or weak against — a pattern you'll see with 弱い and 強い everywhere.

どちら様ですか — the most polite way to ask someone's identity. Not "who are you?" (too blunt) but "which person might you be?" (gracefully indirect). That indirectness is the whole point of どちら: it's どっち with its edges sanded down.

The ど prefix marks it as the question member of the ko-so-a-do set: こちら (this way/this one — near me), そちら (that way — near you), あちら (over there — far from both), どちら (which way?). Use どちら when choosing between two options: どちらがいいですか (which one would you prefer?).

It doubles as a polite "where": どちらからいらっしゃいましたか (where did you come from?) — the kind of thing you'd hear at a reception desk. Instead of directly asking "where," you gently ask "which direction," as if the person is one of several possibilities. The question doesn't demand. It invites.

Japanese forces you to specify two things about siblings that English doesn't: (1) older or younger, and (2) whose sibling — yours or someone else's. There's no neutral "sister." The vocabulary won't let you dodge either question.

姉 (あね) is my older sister, used when talking about her to outsiders. It's the humble form — you never elevate your own family. The polite counterpart is お姉さん (おねえさん), which you'd use for someone else's older sister, or to address your own sister directly at home. Introducing your sister at a formal event as お姉さん would be wrong — it violates the うち／そと principle by honoring your own in-group.

The full sibling map: 姉 (あね, my older sister) / お姉さん (someone's). 兄 (あに, my older brother) / お兄さん. 妹 (いもうと, my younger sister) / 妹さん. 弟 (おとうと, my younger brother) / 弟さん. Birth order is baked into the vocabulary because it determines social dynamics. There is no way around it.

This word has two lives, and mixing them up is one of the most common early mistakes. First life: to memorize — to deliberately put something into your memory. 単語を覚える (memorize vocabulary). 漢字を覚える (learn kanji by heart). Second life: to remember — to hold a memory. あの日のことを覚えている (I remember that day). The ている form is key: 覚えている means "I have it memorized / I remember it" — the ongoing state of storage, not the act of storing.

覚えている vs 思い出す: Both can translate as "remember," but they describe different things. 覚えている — the memory never left; it's been in your head the whole time. 思い出す (おもいだす) — you're recalling something, pulling it back from wherever it had receded to. まだ覚えている (I still remember — it never faded). やっと思い出した (I finally recalled it — it came back to me).

The kanji 覚 has 見 (to see) at the bottom — the eye-on-legs — with components related to awareness on top. To 覚える is to make something stay where your mind can see it.

Particles: 覚える takes を — the thing memorized is the direct object. 名前を覚える. 道を覚える (learn the route).

Potential が覚えられる: が. こんなに多い漢字が覚えられない (I can't memorize this many kanji).

Passive に覚えられる: に marks who remembers you. 先生に名前を覚えられた (the teacher remembered my name).

Causative に…を覚えさせる: に marks who you make memorize, を marks what. 子供に九九を覚えさせる (make the kid memorize the times tables).

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryおぼえるを
Negativeおぼえないを
Politeおぼえますを
Polite negおぼえませんを
Pastおぼえたを
Polite pastおぼえましたを
て-formおぼえてを
Volitionalおぼえようを
Polite volおぼえましょうを
Passiveおぼえられるに
Causativeおぼえさせるに／を
Caus-passおぼえさせられるに
Potentialおぼえられるが
Imperativeおぼえろを
Imp なさいおぼえなさいを
Cond ～ばおぼえればを
Cond ～たらおぼえたらを

この部屋は狭い. If you've seen a Tokyo apartment, you've felt this word. せまい doesn't just mean small — it means the space is too small for what's in it. A room can be 小さい (small) without being 狭い (cramped). 小さい is a neutral measurement. 狭い is a complaint — this should be bigger.

The metaphor extends to personality. 心が狭い (narrow-hearted — petty, ungenerous). 視野が狭い (narrow field of vision — narrow-minded). Cramped thinking, cramped generosity. The opposite is 広い (ひろい, spacious/wide) — and the pair 狭い/広い is distinct from 小さい/大きい. One is about whether a space fits its contents. The other is about absolute size.

Dictionaryせまい
Negativeせまくない
Pastせまかった
Past negせまくなかった
Adverbせまく
て-formせまくて

赤ちゃん — baby. Literally "red one," because newborns come out red-faced. 赤字 — financial deficit, same as English "in the red." 赤の他人 — a complete stranger, where 赤 acts as an intensifier meaning "utter, absolute." That last one is the surprise: 赤 sometimes has nothing to do with color at all. It just means "completely."

The kanji tells a vivid story: 大 (person) over 火 (fire). A person standing over flames, face flushed from the heat. In oracle bone script, it was even more explicit — a figure with the fire right underneath.

赤い belongs to an exclusive club: the handful of basic color words that are true い-adjectives. The full set is 赤い (red), 青い (blue), 白い (white), 黒い (black), 黄色い (yellow). Other colors — green, purple, orange — are nouns and need の to modify things. You can't say 緑い. This five-member club reveals which colors are old enough in Japanese to have earned their own adjective forms.

Dictionaryあかい
Negativeあかくない
Pastあかかった
Past negあかくなかった
Adverbあかく
て-formあかくて

Japanese has no single word for "to wear." Different verbs handle different body zones: 着る for the torso (shirts, jackets, dresses). はく below the waist (pants, skirts, shoes, socks). かぶる on the head (hats, helmets). する or つける for accessories (watches, ties, earrings). Getting dressed isn't one abstract action — it's a series of specific physical motions, and the language tracks each one.

Trap: 着る is ichidan despite ending in -iru and being only two mora. Don't confuse it with 切る (きる, to cut), which is godan. Identical sound, completely different conjugation. 着ない (ichidan negative) vs 切らない (godan negative). Get this wrong and you're saying "I don't cut" when you mean "I don't wear."

The kanji 着 is also "to arrive" — and in compounds, arrival is actually the more common meaning: 到着 (とうちゃく, arrival). A garment "arrives" on your body, settles onto it. For the ongoing state of wearing, use 着ている: 赤いシャツを着ている (is wearing a red shirt). And there's a set phrase worth knowing: 服に着られている — "being worn by the clothes," meaning the outfit overpowers the person, like a kid swimming in an oversized suit.

Particles: 着る takes を — the garment is the direct object. コートを着る. For the ongoing state, use 着ている.

Potential が着られる: が. このドレスがまだ着られる (this dress can still be worn).

Passive に着られる: rare, but 服に着られている (being worn by the clothes) is a set phrase.

Causative に…を着させる: に marks who you dress, を marks the garment. 子供にコートを着させる (make the kid put on a coat).

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryきるを
Negativeきないを
Politeきますを
Polite negきませんを
Pastきたを
Polite pastきましたを
て-formきてを
Volitionalきようを
Polite volきましょうを
Passiveきられるに
Causativeきさせるに／を
Caus-passきさせられるに
Potentialきられるが
Imperativeきろを
Imp なさいきなさいを
Cond ～ばきればを
Cond ～たらきたらを

In internet Japanese, w means "lol." It comes from わらう — the w at the start. More w's = funnier: マジでwww. A long string of w's looks like grass growing, which spawned 草 (くさ, grass) as slang for "that's hilarious" — 草生える (grass is growing — I'm dying). You might encounter this before you encounter the word in speech.

笑う covers both laughing and smiling. Japanese doesn't force you to choose. 大声で笑う (laugh loudly). にっこり笑う (smile gently). Context and adverbs do the distinguishing. If you need "smile" specifically, 微笑む (ほほえむ) exists but it's literary. In conversation, 笑う handles everything.

The kanji 笑 has bamboo (竹) on top and a person bending with arms up (夭) below. Someone doubled over, overcome. Whether the bamboo represents teeth or bending stalks is debated, but the image works either way.

Particles: 笑う is primarily intransitive — you just laugh. みんなが笑った. But it can take を when laughing at someone: 人を笑う (mock someone). This を carries a sting — you're making someone the target.

Passive に笑われる: に marks who laughs at you. みんなに笑われた (everyone laughed at me). Classic adversative passive.

Causative を笑わせる: を when making someone laugh: お客を笑わせる (make the audience laugh).

五段 Godan自動詞（を also possible）
Dictionaryわらうを
Negativeわらわないを
Politeわらいますを
Polite negわらいませんを
Pastわらったを
Polite pastわらいましたを
て-formわらってを
Volitionalわらおうを
Polite volわらいましょうを
Passiveわらわれるに
Causativeわらわせるを／に
Caus-passわらわせられるに
Potentialわらえるを
Imperativeわらえを
Imp なさいわらいなさいを
Cond ～ばわらえばを
Cond ～たらわらったらを

English builds superlatives with -est or "most." Japanese doesn't bother. Slap 一番 in front of any adjective and you're done. 一番大きい (biggest). 一番好き (favorite — literally "#1 liked"). 一番いい (best). No irregular forms, no memorization. This is one of those places where Japanese is genuinely simpler than English.

番 means number/turn/order — it's a counter for ordinal positions. 二番 (second), 三番 (third). It also means "turn" as in sequence: 次は私の番だ (it's my turn next). And 番号 (ばんごう) means "number" as in phone number or seat number.

一番 works both as a noun and an adverb, no particle needed when modifying. As a noun: 一番になりたい (I want to be number one). As an adverb: 一番高い山 (the tallest mountain). It goes directly before the adjective it intensifies — no の, no に, just stack them.

授業をサボる — to skip class. サボる comes from French "sabotage," turned into a Japanese verb. This might be the most useful phrase in student vocabulary. 授業 means class as a scheduled event — the block of time when teaching happens. 授業が始まる (class starts). 授業中 (during class — a state that means "do not disturb"). It's not the group of students (that's クラス) or a university course over a semester (that's 講義 (こうぎ)).

The kanji reveal an attitude: 授 (to grant, bestow) + 業 (work, deed). Knowledge handed down, top to bottom. That same 業 appears in 卒業 (そつぎょう, graduation — completing your work) and 職業 (しょくぎょう, occupation). Serious, sustained effort — never casual.

今週 (this week), 来週 (next week), 先週 (last week), 毎週 (every week). Four compounds, one pattern. The same prefixes — 今, 来, 先, 毎 — snap onto 月 (month) and 年 (year) too. Learn the prefixes once, unlock the whole time-reference system.

On its own, 週 rarely stands alone. It lives in compounds. When counting weeks, add 間: 一週間 (いっしゅうかん, one week), 二週間 (two weeks). Without 間, 一週 means "one lap/cycle" — and in fact 一周 (using the base kanji 周) means exactly that: one circuit around something.

The kanji 週 is the movement radical 辶 wrapped around 周 (circumference, cycle). A cycle that moves forward — one revolution of days, then the next begins. Time going around and around.

You're learning 漢字 to learn 漢字. The word is self-referential — two characters explaining themselves.

漢 refers to the Han dynasty, so culturally dominant that "Han" became synonymous with "Chinese" across East Asia. 字 means "character." Han characters. But here's the irony: kanji aren't Japanese. They were imported from China starting around the 5th century, and Japanese speakers performed one of the greatest hacks in linguistic history — taking a writing system designed for a completely unrelated language and retrofitting it onto their own. That's why each kanji has 音読み (おんよみ, the Chinese-derived reading) and 訓読み (くんよみ, the native Japanese reading). The Chinese sound came with the character; the Japanese meaning got bolted on later.

Every December, Kiyomizu Temple in Kyoto announces the 今年の漢字 — a single character chosen to capture the national mood of the year. Millions voluntarily take the 漢字検定 (かんじけんてい, Kanji Aptitude Test). Kanji aren't just a writing tool in Japan. They're a cultural institution people have opinions about and emotional attachments to.

Japan has roughly 70 million bicycles — nearly one for every two people. The ママチャリ (mama-chari) — a utilitarian city bike with a basket and child seat — is an icon of Japanese daily life. チャリ is the casual slang for bicycle, shortened from チャリンコ (possibly from the sound of a bicycle bell). In actual conversation, almost nobody says the full じてんしゃ unless the situation calls for some formality.

But the formal word rewards you with a perfect kanji breakdown: 自 (self) + 転 (revolve) + 車 (vehicle). A self-revolving vehicle — you are the engine. Compare with 自動車 (じどうしゃ, automobile): 自 (self) + 動 (move) + 車 (vehicle). The difference between 転 (revolving by your effort) and 動 (moving on its own) is the entire distinction between a bicycle and a car, captured in one kanji swap.

If you live in Japan, 電車に乗る (ride a train), 電車を降りる (get off), and 電車に乗り遅れる (miss the train) become part of your daily operating system. Trains aren't transportation. They're infrastructure you build your life around.

電車 specifically means the electric urban/suburban trains that run on tracks with overhead power lines. Strictly speaking, the 新幹線 (bullet train) is a 列車 (れっしゃ, a "lined-up vehicle"), and rural diesel trains are 汽車 (きしゃ). But in everyday Tokyo conversation, 電車 covers most train situations.

The 電 kanji — 雨 (rain) with a bolt of lightning through it — originally meant lightning and was repurposed for the modern concept of electricity. It spawns a whole constellation of modern-life words: 電話 (でんわ, telephone — electric talk), 電気 (でんき, electricity/light), 電子 (でんし, electronic). Anything powered by electricity gets this kanji.

探す is the process. 見つける is the result. You 探す (search), and if you're lucky, you 見つける (find). 仕事を探している (I'm looking for a job — still searching, no luck yet). 言葉を探す (searching for the right words — groping for what to say).

The kanji 探 has the hand radical 扌 and a right side that originally depicted reaching deep into a hole. Your hand in the dark, fumbling for something you know is there. That physical groping is the feeling 探す carries — active, sometimes frustrated effort.

There's a separate kanji 捜す (same pronunciation) for more serious searching — police investigations, missing persons. In casual writing, 探す handles both. The distinction mostly surfaces in formal or news contexts.

Particles: 探す takes を — the thing you search for is the direct object. 鍵を探す (look for the keys). 新しいアパートを探す.

Potential が探せる: が. ネットで何でも探せる (you can search for anything online).

Passive に探される: に marks the searcher. 警察に探されている (being searched for by police).

Causative に…を探させる: に marks who searches, を marks what. 部下に資料を探させる (have a subordinate look for the documents).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryさがすを
Negativeさがさないを
Politeさがしますを
Polite negさがしませんを
Pastさがしたを
Polite pastさがしましたを
て-formさがしてを
Volitionalさがそうを
Polite volさがしましょうを
Passiveさがされるに
Causativeさがさせるに／を
Caus-passさがさせられるに
Potentialさがせるが
Imperativeさがせを
Imp なさいさがしなさいを
Cond ～ばさがせばを
Cond ～たらさがしたらを

かみ is one of three words with the same sound: 紙 (paper), 神 (god), 髪 (hair). Three completely different concepts, identical pronunciation. Context always disambiguates — 紙をください will never be mistaken for a request for gods — but the triple homophone is a favorite source of Japanese wordplay and puns.

Japan has a deep relationship with paper. 和紙 (わし, Japanese paper), handmade from mulberry bark fibers, is a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. It's the material behind 障子 (shoji screens), calligraphy, and 折り紙 (おりがみ — literally "folding paper," 折る + 紙). When a material gets this embedded in a culture, the language builds a whole vocabulary around it.

The kanji 紙 has the thread/silk radical 糸 — because the earliest paper in China was made from plant fibers, silk rags, and old fishing nets. Paper is woven from threads. The fiber connection lives in the radical.

歌を歌う — to sing a song. Yes, the same kanji appears twice. In Japanese, this repetition sounds natural, not redundant. カラオケで歌う is practically a national pastime — the word カラオケ itself is Japanese (空 empty + オーケストラ orchestra), one of Japan's most successful cultural exports.

In ancient Japan, singing and poetry were the same art. 万葉集, the oldest poetry anthology, is a collection of 歌. 短歌 (たんか, short poems) and 和歌 (わか, Japanese poems) use this same character. Poems were meant to be voiced, not silently read. The older literary verb 詠う (also うたう) specifically means to recite poetry — a relic of that merged tradition.

The kanji 歌 is 可 doubled (repeated sounds) plus 欠 — a person with their mouth wide open. Someone making repeated sounds with an open mouth. That 欠 radical appears in other "mouth open" kanji: 飲 (to drink), 吹 (to blow).

Particles: 歌う takes を — the song is the direct object. この歌を歌って (sing this song).

Potential が歌える: が. この曲が歌える？ (can you sing this?).

Passive に歌われる: に marks the singer. 多くの人に歌われている (sung by many people).

Causative に…を歌わせる: に marks who sings, を marks the song. 生徒に国歌を歌わせる (make the students sing the national anthem).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryうたうを
Negativeうたわないを
Politeうたいますを
Polite negうたいませんを
Pastうたったを
Polite pastうたいましたを
て-formうたってを
Volitionalうたおうを
Polite volうたいましょうを
Passiveうたわれるに
Causativeうたわせるに／を
Caus-passうたわせられるに
Potentialうたえるが
Imperativeうたえを
Imp なさいうたいなさいを
Cond ～ばうたえばを
Cond ～たらうたったらを

tags: 速い vs 遅い pair
lesson:
電車が遅い — is the train slow, or is the train late? Both. 遅い refuses to distinguish between the two. The train isn't arriving at the expected time, and whether that's because it's crawling or because it departed behind schedule, the result is the same. English splits this into two concepts. Japanese sees one.

遅刻 (ちこく, being tardy) carries real weight in Japan, where punctuality isn't a suggestion. If a meeting starts at 10:00, arriving at 10:01 is 遅刻. The word itself is 遅 (late) + 刻 (time/moment) — late to the moment.

遅い vs 遅れる: 遅い is the adjective — something is slow or late. 遅れる (おくれる) is the verb — to become late, to fall behind. 電車が遅い = the train is inherently slow. 電車が遅れている = the train is running late (something went wrong). One describes a quality, the other describes an event.

Dictionaryおそい
Negativeおそくない
Pastおそかった
Past negおそくなかった
Adverbおそく
て-formおそくて

If your boss mentions 首, pay attention. 首になった means "I got fired" — literally "became a neck." The image is decapitation: your head gets separated from the body of the company. 首にする (to fire someone). It's vivid, common, and the first thing most people think of when they hear くび in a work context.

The kanji 首 was originally a pictograph of a head, not a neck — a face with hair on top. That older "head" meaning dominates in compounds: 首都 (しゅと, capital city — head city), 首相 (しゅしょう, prime minister — head minister). In modern standalone use, though, くび has shifted down to mean the neck — the part connecting head to body.

Idioms: 首を長くして待つ (wait with a long neck — wait eagerly, craning to see). 首をかしげる (tilt your neck — be puzzled). 首を突っ込む (stick your neck in — poke your nose into someone else's business). The neck turns out to be one of the most productive body parts for figurative language.

はやい has two kanji, and they mean different things. 速い is about speed — how fast something moves. 早い is about time — how early something happens. 車が速い (the car is fast). 朝が早い (the morning is early). They sound identical in speech, and Japanese people themselves sometimes write the wrong one.

The distinction can blur. 返事が速い (the reply is fast — it came quickly) vs 返事が早い (the reply is early — it came ahead of schedule). Both are valid, slightly different angles. When you're unsure which kanji to use, 早い is the safer default — it's used more broadly and covers more ground.

The kanji 速 has the movement radical 辶 — the same one in 遅い (slow). A road beneath the character, motion built in. Its opposite 遅い uses the same radical with a different payload: speed and slowness are both about movement.

Dictionaryはやい
Negativeはやくない
Pastはやかった
Past negはやくなかった
Adverbはやく
て-formはやくて

一緒に行こう — let's go together. 一緒に食べない？ — want to eat together? These are among the most common invitations in Japanese, and 一緒に is the word that makes them warm. Without it, you're just stating plans. With it, you're including someone.

The kanji: 一 (one) + 緒 (thread). Things bound by a single thread — unified. The に makes it an adverb modifying the verb. Without に, 一緒 works as a な-adjective: 同じ学校で一緒だった (we were at the same school together).

In casual speech, 一緒 also means "the same": それ、一緒じゃない？ (isn't that the same thing?). This overlaps with 同じ, but 一緒 feels more colloquial — friends, not papers. Two things on the same thread are effectively one.

今 (now) + 月 (month) = this month. Swap the prefix: 先月 (せんげつ, last month), 来月 (らいげつ, next month), 毎月 (まいつき, every month). Same prefixes work with 週 and 年. One set of prefixes, every time unit — this is one of the most systematic corners of Japanese.

The reading to notice: 月 becomes げつ here, not つき or がつ. Three readings, three contexts. がつ for specific months (一月, 二月...). つき for the standalone word "moon" or "month." げつ for relative time and duration. 今月, 来月, 先月 all use げつ.

明日遊ばない？ doesn't mean "want to play?" in a childish way. It means "want to hang out?" Adults use 遊ぶ constantly. Kids at the park? 遊ぶ. Saturday with friends? 遊ぶ. Goofing off instead of working? 遊ぶ. Going on a trip? 遊ぶ. Any leisure activity that isn't work or duty falls under this word.

遊びに行く (to go out for fun / to go visit someone) is the standard way to describe going to a friend's place. うちに遊びに来てね (come hang out at my place). The に after 遊び marks purpose — you're going in order to hang out.

And one pointed usage: あの人に遊ばれた — "I was played by that person." Romantic context: they led you on, toyed with your feelings. The passive form turns leisure into cruelty.

Particles: 遊ぶ is intransitive — no を needed. Use で for where: 公園で遊ぶ. Use と for who: 友達と遊ぶ.

Passive に遊ばれる: に marks who toyed with you. あの人に遊ばれた (I was played by that person).

Causative を遊ばせる: を when letting someone play: 子供を外で遊ばせる (let the kids play outside).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryあそぶで／と
Negativeあそばないで／と
Politeあそびますで／と
Polite negあそびませんで／と
Pastあそんだで／と
Polite pastあそびましたで／と
て-formあそんでで／と
Volitionalあそぼうで／と
Polite volあそびましょうで／と
Passiveあそばれるに

Caus-passあそばせられるに

Imp なさいあそびなさいで／と

Cond ～たらあそんだらで／と

Spelling trap: とおい, not とうい. The middle sound is a true お, not う. This is one of the few common words where the long お sound is written おお instead of the usual おう — a spelling irregularity shared with 大きい (おおきい). Just memorize these as exceptions.

遠い works for every kind of distance. Physical: 駅は遠い (the station is far). Temporal: 遠い昔 (the distant past). Social: 遠い親戚 (a distant relative). Visual: 目が遠い (far-sighted). The opposite 近い (ちかい, near) maps to the same domains: 近い親戚 (close relative), 近い将来 (near future). Learn them as a pair.
